perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Recipient will endeavor not to disclose and will try to keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who may need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will generally inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will attempt to ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will endeavor to be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein will be generally observed and will assume liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents to a reasonable extent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strict obligation to maintain confidentiality. The modified text introduces ambiguity through words like 'endeavor,' 'try,' 'generally,' 'attempt,' and 'to a reasonable extent.' This weakens the obligation and makes enforcement difficult, as it's unclear what level of effort is sufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4(1) of the EU Trade Secrets Directive requires member states to ensure that rightholders have legal means to prevent the unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret. By weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality, the modified text undermines the directive's objective of providing strong legal protection for trade secrets, as the 'endeavor' and 'try' aspects do not provide enough assurance against unlawful disclosure, potentially hindering the rightsholder's ability to seek effective remedies.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using words like 'endeavor,' 'try,' 'generally,' 'attempt,' and 'to a reasonable extent,' weakening the original strict obligation to maintain confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that Article 4(1) of the EU Trade Secrets Directive requires member states to ensure legal means to prevent unlawful disclosure. By weakening the confidentiality obligation, the modified text undermines this objective, failing to provide adequate assurance against unlawful disclosure. The absence of relevant law snippets does not negate this contradiction, as the LLM-generated Law Explanation clearly articulates the directive's requirement for strong legal protection of trade secrets, which is directly contradicted by the ambiguous language introduced in the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, d e s i g n , p r o c e s s , procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, s p e c i f i c a t i o n s , software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information may generally mean data or proprietary information that the Discloser considers confidential, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including potentially: (i) scientific or technical information, inventions, designs, processes, procedures, formulas, improvements, technology or methods; (ii) concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) other information that the Discloser subjectively believes should be recognized as Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines Confidential Information with a list of objective criteria. The modified version uses terms like 'may generally mean', 'considers confidential', 'potentially' and 'subjectively believes'. These changes make the definition of confidential information vague and dependent on the Discloser's subjective opinion rather than clear, objective criteria. This makes it difficult to determine what is actually protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1(4) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The modified text, by making the definition of confidential information subjective and dependent on the Discloser's opinion, could fail to meet the requirements that the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known because of the vagueness it introduced.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into the definition of 'Confidential Information,' making it subjective and dependent on the Discloser's opinion. The Law Explanation states that this vagueness could cause the agreement to fail the requirements of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act because it may not ensure the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known. Although neither scraped snippet is valid, the contradiction is supported by the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation that state that because of the changes in the modified text, the definition of 'Confidential Information' is ambiguous and dependent on the Discloser's subjective opinion. This ambiguity weakens the protection of trade secrets because it's not clear what's protected.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Recipient will use the Confidential Information exclusively for the permitted purpose stated in clause 2 and not use the information for its own purposes or benefit.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Recipient will generally use the Confidential Information for purposes related to the purpose stated in clause 2 and may use the information for its own purposes or benefit, provided it makes reasonable efforts to compensate the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause strictly prohibits the Recipient from using confidential information for their own benefit. The modified clause introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection by allowing such use if 'reasonable efforts' are made to compensate the Discloser. The definition of 'reasonable efforts' is not provided, and may cause issues with compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10 of the Trade Secrets Act 2018 prohibits the unauthorized use or disclosure of a trade secret. The amended clause allows the recipient to use confidential information for their own purposes which creates a ambiguity and also goes against the intention to protect trade secrets under the act.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the prohibition against using confidential information for the recipient's own benefit. While the original text strictly forbids this, the changed text allows it with 'reasonable efforts' to compensate the Discloser. This introduces ambiguity and potentially undermines the protection of trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports that Section 10 of the Trade Secrets Act 2018 prohibits the unauthorized use or disclosure of a trade secret, however, without law snippets this relies on the explanation provided. Because the definition of 'reasonable efforts' is not defined, it may cause issues with compensation.
